main_ingredients: sauerkraut / fermented cabbage (kimchi or Chinese-style pickled cabbage)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hot plate serving of pork belly with sauerkraut is approximately 300 g, comprising sliced pork belly and fermented cabbage served on a sizzling iron plate.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, belly (side), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10006); supplemented with USDA data for sauerkraut (NDB 11439) and estimated composite for restaurant preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for hot-plate pork belly with sauerkraut exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly (NDB 10006) and USDA sauerkraut (NDB 11439), weighted approximately 70% pork belly and 30% sauerkraut by mass, adjusted for cooking losses and added condiments (sesame oil, soy sauce, garlic). This is a composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous food reference for pork and fermented vegetable dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot-plate pork belly with sauerkraut. GI is estimated at 32 based on the analogous food profile: predominantly pork belly (GI effectively 0 for pure protein/fat) combined with fermented cabbage (very low GI, ~15) and minor condiments. The composite dish is very low in digestible carbohydrate (3.2 g per 100 g), yielding a low GI estimate of approximately 32 consistent with mixed pork-vegetable dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (32 × 9.6 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.1 g, classified low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sauerkraut fermentation and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less per meal, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1440 mg sodium — exceeding 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced soy sauce and fermented cabbage to lower sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low digestible carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated glycemic index of 32, this dish has a low glycemic load of approximately 3.1 per serving and is unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursions, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and high sodium from fermented cabbage and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork belly is a high-fat cut with approximately 5.8 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (≤150 g), choose leaner pork cuts where possible, and be mindful of the high sodium content which contributes to blood pressure elevation and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Plate Pork Belly with Sauerkraut (石板烤酸菜三层肉; Korean: 철판 돼지삼겹살 김치찜) is a Korean-influenced sizzling plate dish popular in Singapore's Korean restaurant scene. It features thick slices of pork belly (samgyeopsal) — a fatty, layered cut from the underside of the pig — grilled or pan-seared and then combined with sauerkraut or kimchi-style fermented cabbage on a hot iron plate. The dish is typically seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and sometimes gochujang (Korean chilli paste), and served sizzling at the table. The texture contrasts tender, slightly charred pork with tangy, crunchy fermented cabbage. Nutritionally, the dish is high in fat (approximately 16.5 g per 100 g) and protein (13.5 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (estimated GI ~32). The fermentation process in the sauerkraut contributes probiotics and vitamin C but also significantly elevates sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g), which is a primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for halal diets. It is a good source of B vitamins (B1, B3, B6, B12) and zinc from the pork component. In Singapore, this dish is served at Korean bibimbap and Korean BBQ restaurants and is commonly ordered as a main course alongside rice or noodles.

meal_context: Served with steamed rice or noodles

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly and sauerkraut data with a weighted composite model. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate profile of the dish and comparable entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy NDB 10006 (pork belly, raw) and NDB 11439 (sauerkraut, canned), weighted 70:30 by mass with adjustments for cooking losses and added condiments (sesame oil, soy sauce, garlic). No direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite preparation in the source databases. 3. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy pork belly entry and cannot be reliably estimated for the composite. 4. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used. 5. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used. 6. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the composite estimation source. 7. GI is estimated at 32 based on analogous low-carbohydrate pork-vegetable dish profiles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found. 8. The dish is served at Mincheng Bibimbap (민정비빔밥 敏丁拌) at Funan, Singapore, per field survey source data. 9. The Chinese name 石板烤酸菜三层肉 is preserved from the input record and reflects the restaurant's own naming convention. 10. This dish is not halal due to pork content.
